executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment continues its post-pandemic normalization trajectory, with 74.9 million enrollees across 51 reporting states as of February 2026. Year-over-year enrollment declined across all major states, ranging from minimal decreases in North Carolina (0.0%) to more substantial reductions in Michigan (-7.6%) and California (-6.8%). Texas experienced a moderate 3.8% enrollment decline to approximately 4.0 million beneficiaries, positioning the state as the third-largest Medicaid program nationally. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national per-enrollee average of $2,883. Texas demonstrates notable cost efficiency at $3,014 per enrollee, significantly below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Massachusetts ($4,221), though above the most efficient programs in Florida ($2,036) and North Carolina ($2,623).

Quality performance reveals concerning disparities across states, with national average scores at 65.6 out of 100 points. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a moderate quality score of 62, trailing leading states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) by substantial margins. This performance gap represents a critical opportunity for improvement, particularly given Texas's relatively efficient spending patterns. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened oversight focus on state directed payments, community engagement requirements, and provider enrollment moratoria, requiring continued attention to compliance and program integrity measures across all state operations.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with nearly 4 million enrollees, yet its performance reveals a mixed picture of cost efficiency and quality challenges. The state's per-enrollee expenditure of $3,014 sits just above the national average of $2,883, positioning it favorably against peer states like Ohio ($3,584) and Pennsylvania ($4,363), though notably higher than fellow non-expansion state Florida ($2,036). Texas demonstrates strong administrative capabilities with a 93.3% managed care penetration rate, well above the national average of 83.2%, indicating sophisticated delivery system management. The program's -3.8% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns closely with national unwinding trends, performing better than large states like California (-6.8%) and Michigan (-7.6%), suggesting relatively stable post-pandemic transitions.

However, Texas faces significant quality performance challenges that warrant immediate attention. With a quality score of 62/100, ranking 30th nationally, the state falls into the "moderate" tier but trails concerning peer comparisons—particularly given that fellow large states and southern neighbors show varied performance patterns. The state's quality metrics place it closer to bottom-tier performers like Arkansas (54) than top-performing states, representing a critical vulnerability for a program serving nearly 4 million Texans. Areas to monitor include the ongoing federal regulatory environment around community engagement requirements and state-directed payments, which could impact both program financing and enrollment patterns. The combination of high managed care penetration with middling quality outcomes suggests Texas has the infrastructure foundation for improvement but needs targeted interventions to translate administrative efficiency into better health outcomes for its substantial Medicaid population.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Medicaid spending 51% above national average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,363 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,883, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Florida spending critically low at $2,036 per enrollee
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating inadequate care funding despite having 3.6M enrollees.

alerts.title: Five states show critically low quality scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi have quality scores below 55, well under the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: High spending states show mixed quality outcomes
alerts.description: Pennsylvania has the highest per-enrollee spending ($4,363) but quality data is missing, while Massachusetts combines high spending ($4,221) with top quality scores (81).

alerts.title: Nationwide enrollment moratorium signals major policy shift
alerts.description: Recent Federal Register announcements indicate temporary enrollment moratoria across Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP programs, which could significantly impact future enrollment trends.

executiveInsights.title: Texas Quality Score Below National Average, Risk for Value-Based Contracts
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, significantly below the national average of 65.6 and well under high-performer threshold of 75. This places Texas in the 'moderate' tier with potential quality improvement needs.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality performance could trigger CMS sanctions, impact federal funding, and create opportunities for Accenture to propose quality improvement initiatives. Value-based contract negotiations may be affected by current performance gaps.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement opportunities to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: Texas Enrollment Declining 3.8% YoY, Affecting Revenue Base
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped to 3,997,752 enrollees with a -3.8% year-over-year decline. This follows the national trend but represents significant membership loss impacting program scale.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Declining enrollment reduces the revenue base for managed care plans and may signal post-PHE unwinding impacts requiring operational adjustments. Accenture should assess capacity planning and cost structure implications for HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment forecasting models with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas Per-Enrollee Costs Above National Average at $3,014
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $3,014 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,883, ranking among higher-cost states. Total expenditures reach $12.0B annually for the program.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-member costs indicate potential efficiency opportunities and budget pressures for HHSC. This cost differential creates urgency for value-based care initiatives and administrative cost reduction strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost benchmarking analysis to HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: CMS Announcing Provider Enrollment Moratoria - Compliance Risk
executiveInsights.summary: Recent Federal Register notices show CMS implementing nationwide temporary moratoria on provider enrollment across Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP programs. Multiple notices published in May 2026 indicate heightened oversight.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Provider network adequacy could be impacted by enrollment restrictions, requiring HHSC to adjust network management strategies. Accenture should help HHSC prepare for potential provider access issues and compliance requirements.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review provider network impact assessment with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas Managed Care Penetration Above National at 93.3%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M enrollees, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care adoption in the state.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration positions Texas well for value-based initiatives but increases dependency on MCO performance. Accenture should leverage this strength in proposing advanced managed care optimization strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review MCO performance optimization opportunities with HHSC

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% YoY, potentially signaling unwinding pressures that could stress system capacity and create operational gaps.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop flexible capacity models and proactive enrollment retention tools.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality performance gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with a 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in value-based contracts and federal oversight.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Launch comprehensive quality improvement initiative targeting top-tier performance metrics.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration suggests potential large-scale procurement cycles that could reshape vendor landscape.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position for major MCO technology and operations RFPs through enhanced partnership strategies.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal regulatory shifts
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple CMS policy changes including community engagement requirements and payment parameters create compliance complexity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated regulatory monitoring and rapid compliance response capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: CMS nationwide temporary moratorium on provider enrollment creates network adequacy challenges and system integration needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop alternative provider credentialing and network management solutions.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight docket indicates heightened scrutiny requiring enhanced compliance and audit readiness.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen audit trail capabilities and proactive compliance monitoring systems.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $3,014 per-enrollee cost exceeds national average, presenting optimization opportunities while managing budget pressures.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement advanced analytics-driven cost management and utilization optimization programs.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive market pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas procurement portal activity and multi-state RFP patterns suggest increased competition for state Medicaid technology contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Accelerate differentiated solution development and strengthen state relationship management.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment stands at 3,997,752 beneficiaries as of February 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Enrollment Change
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.8% year-over-year, slightly better than the national trend.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $3,014 per Medicaid enrollee annually, above the national average of $2,883.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees, well above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas quality score of 62 ranks 30th nationally, in the moderate performance tier below the national average of 65.6.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement sources identified: HHSC portal and Electronic State Business Daily.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Eleven federal regulatory and OIG items identified affecting Medicaid operations nationwide.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in available data.
